For example, textiles and chemicals should have an interoperable data model. Furthermore, the category specific product requirements would be based on the same data model for a specific delegated act. For example, if there is a delegated act for ‘textiles’, an associated standard data model should be available. This data model should align to the extent possible with other relevant data models. a. In case the use of specific data models is imposed by sector-specific delegated acts, it is assumed that interoperability mechanisms are in place to enable semantic interoperability between sectors and between different sector-specific data models. 8. Access to DPP data using the weblink will require, in most cases, a redirection service, that the REO has control over, able to process a unique product identifier or weblink and find the ‘active’ weblink. In addition, in most cases, a (set of) DPP data service(s) that the REO has control over is required, to process an ‘active’ weblink and produce as an output DPP data, potentially considering among other things, the access rights, data format, and multiple locations of DPP data. 9. A ‘default’ redirection service is made available. Consider the case that only a unique product identifier is available (and no functioning weblink). To ensure access to DPP data using the unique product identifier we assume a ‘default’ redirection service that is able to take the unique product identifier as an input, and produce the active weblink as an output. Active means that by using this weblink the DPP data can be found. In case the REO no longer makes DPP data available (e.g. due 90 to insolvency), the ‘active’ weblink may have to be changed in the ‘default’ redirection service to ensure that the weblink now points to backed-up DPP data. 10. Should the data carrier not contain a web link, it is understood that dedicated means will be defined to construct one (e.g., a professional application used exclusively in B2B contexts or a DPP link search portal). 11. A unique product identifier corresponds to the finest level of granularity present in any attribute of the DPP data (which may combine model, batch, and item level attributes). Therefore, there must be at least as many unique product identifiers as there are instances at that finest granularity level. For example, if any attribute in the DPP data requires item-level detail, each individual product must have a unique product identifier. It is possible, though not yet determined, that unique product identifiers may be required at a finer granularity than the DPP data itself (e.g., item-level identifiers even when all other DPP data is at model or batch level). USER STORIES USER STORY 1: CREATE A DPP 12. Next to the unique identifiers and commodity code, other relevant data encoded in the data carrier may also be stored in the EU registry (Art. 13). 13. Public Authorities ensure availability of a service (e.g. by providing the service themselves or ensuring that others provide it) via which a standard data model for products regulated via a delegated act is made available. Making available here means at least that it can be requested from the relevant authorities’ server in a standard machine readable format (e.g. SHACL, CSV, JSON, XML, etc.). 14. Public Authorities ensure availability of a service via which a DPP (data model) can be assessed for its compliance. This does not qualify as an assessment of the accuracy of data in the DPP but it may be an assessment of the data model and completeness of the data. 15. Public Authorities ensure availability of a service (e.g. by providing the service themselves or ensuring that others, such as Issuing agencies, provide it) via which a request can be made for a unique operator identifier and a unique facility identifier (based on ESPR art. 12). 16. Voluntary data, including obtained certifications, may be added to the DPP by the REO. 17. REOs may voluntarily create unique product identifiers at a lower granularity level (batch/item). For example, if lower granularity is beneficial (e.g., for optimisation of the REO’s business processes), 18. Several repositories may be used to store the data corresponding to a single DPP. Indeed, it is common industry practice that companies store data applicable at model, batch and item levels on different servers. 19. In the short term, multiple IAA systems will exist in parallel, for example to allow access for reading and writing a DPP. Long term, using eIDAS 2.0 compliant IDs and claims about roles (e.g. repairer, recycler) as the default would be desirable to reduce administrative burden. 20. The DPP back-up provider should be able to provide the same level of access controls as the REO, thus the access control (i.e. which parties get access to which data) is shared with the DPP back- up provider. USER STORY 2: LINK A DPP 21. We assume that a ‘new’ DPP and an ‘old’ DPP exist, and that they can be linked (i.e., for instance that the data model contains a field for such links). This link has a start date, which could be the same as the creation of the product, and potentially an end date as well. 91 22. Copying the old DPP data into the new DPP is allowed, however, this would only constitute a “link” if the copied data is kept up-to-date. Namely, we assume that any changes to the old DPP should be reflected in the new DPP. (e.g. information about recall / safety / deactivation) 23. A new DPP should reference an old DPP, but the old DPP does not have to reference a new one. 24. ‘Linking’ a DPP in the case of a repair during which a component with its own DPP is replaced, is not part of this user story. Instead, this is considered an update to an existing DPP. 25. A potential consequence of such linkage is that the EU registry will contain ‘duplicate/ghost DPPs’ of products that are no longer in circulation and have either 1) become a part of a new product 2) been separated into their components, which have become parts of new products. However, the original DPPs will still be maintained. For example, my car has been stripped and is no longer functional, some of the parts of my car that were functional are placed on a remanufactured car. The old DPP is referenced in the new DPP. However, the old DPP might very well remain available, for example, when the REO is not aware that the original car is no longer functional. So the DPP might still be found in the EU registry, the Web Portal, at the place of purchase online, etc. We are unable to come up with a solution that prevents this and results in an acceptable administrative burden for REOs. 26. It is not mandatory to remove / overwrite the existing data carrier. USER STORY 3: ACCESS DPP DATA VIA DATA CARRIER 27. There may be multiple data formats and data models that the services have to be designed to be compatible with. 28. The user has a device available to scan the data carrier. For some product groups, the user needs to have an application that supports certain standards to scan a data carrier. 29. If the user needs additional credentials, the user is able to determine who the relevant Credentials Agency is. i.e. the knowledge about how to identify the relevant Credentials Agency should be publicly and readily made available. USER STORY 4: ACCESS DPP DATA ONLINE 30. The online seller has received appropriate copies of the data carriers and unique product identifiers as required under Article 10(3). 31. (optional) DPPSP can provide the functionality to compare between DPPs and the data therein. This implies that DPPSP have a means to identify all, or a significant portion, of DPPs placed on the EU market, so that they can provide a comparison service. 32. In accordance with ESPR article 14, a web portal is made available by the Commission which supports comparison between the data in different DPPs. USER STORY 5: ACCESS A COLLECTION OF DPPS 33. Other roles (e.g. End User, REO, Independent Operator) may also make use of the web portal to request a collection of DPPs through a single request. For this user story, we choose to focus on the perspective of a Public Authority doing so. 34. The Public Authorities may want to monitor compliance in which the starting point is a list of unique identifiers. 35. Similarly, the Public Authorities may want to monitor compliance of DPPs on a specific category (e.g. a specific economic operator, a product group, etc.). We assume that they use the web portal for this purpose. 92 36. Features for filtering and selecting are made available as part of the web portal. Enough data is available in the web portal for Public Authorities to be able to filter down to the DPPs they intend to monitor (e.g. REO, Date of DPP creation, etc.). 37. Public Authorities may have access to analytics services that aid them in their compliance duties, for instance by assessing whether they received the DPP data that they requested. USER STORY 6: UPDATE A BACKED-UP DPP 38. The REO is in business and the DPP is active. 39. Access control for the restricted data should be ensured even after insolvency of the REO. I.e. it should not be the case that all data is made public. 40. The REO and DPPSP make an agreement about how data in the back-up is updated USER STORY 7: UPDATE DPP DATA WITHOUT AUTHORIZATION 41. There is no business agreement between the independent operator (e.g. repairer) and the REO. This means that the Independent Operator has no specific access credentials to add repair information. 42. The data added by the Independent Operator does not invalidate the structure of the DPP, the DPP will still conform to the requirements as specified in the relevant delegated act and ESPR article 10 after the update. 43. The contents of the data may be free text, a link or structured data: the requirements and limitations are dependent on the system hosting the data and the requirements on the DPP from the relevant delegated act. 44. Given that the DPP system shall be decentralized, the data added may be hosted by the REO, or by the Independent Operator, or by a third party. In any case, a system to find the added data, given REO’s DPP, must exist. For example, it could be implemented by a specialized search engine over different hosting systems of such data: searching for a DPP could find both the DPP data provided by REO as well as data added by Independent Operators, connected to that DPP. 45. Any component being installed or removed during repair may have its own DPP that needs to be linked to the product’s DPP. In cases the components have a DPP, this can be utilized when recording the repair operation for the product’s DPP. 46. All products subject to data additions are governed by a delegated act requiring item level product identifiers for all products place in the EU market. The product in this story already has an item level product identifier. 47. An Independent Operator may have their own DPP system that performs functionalities relevant to them. 48. There is a risk of spam / incorrect data being added and linked to the DPP. 49. It may be beneficial to the REO to be able to add their own comments / information to the data added by the Independent Operator. For example, a note that the added information is irrelevant/false. This could be performed in a similar manner as the Operator adds the data in the first place. 50. It may be beneficial to set up specialized search / hosting services requiring authorization from the Independent Operator willing to add data, and thus having a potentially higher level of trust than other similar systems. Such systems can be set up by the REO, a third party on behalf of the REO, or an independent third party. 93 51. Allowing independent operators to update a DPP without authorization allows freedom of choice to consumers and prevents REO’s from exercising undesired behaviour. USER STORY 8: UPDATE DPP DATA WITH AUTHORIZATION 52. A business agreement exists between the independent operator (repairer) and the REO or the repair is done by the REO. This means that the Independent Operator (repairer) has appropriate access credentials to add repair information, but not to alter any of the original DPP data. 53. REOs are allowed to make contractual agreements with independent operators that provides these independent operators with certain rights that other independent operators do not have. 54. If I have authorization, I can make specific additions to the DPP directly (I have write access). 55. Given that the DPP system shall be decentralized, the data added by the Independent Operator may be hosted by the REO, or by the Independent Operator, or a third party. If hosted by the Independent Operator, a link to the data must be included in the REO’s DPP. 56. The product already has an item level product identifier. 57. This user story assumes that any component being installed or removed during repair may have its own DPP that needs to be linked to the product’s DPP. In cases the components have a DPP, this can be utilized when recording the repair operation in the product’s DPP. 58. An Independent Operator may have their own DPP system that performs functionalities relevant to them USER STORY 9: UPDATE DPP AT PRODUCT END-OF-LIFE 59. This user story is independent of any sorting, dismantling, and/or shredding process (which may precede this user story). It assumes that the earlier sorting operation has prepared a batch of products to be forwarded to the recycling operator. 60. It is assumed that remanufacturers might have, in some cases, the necessity to destroy the data carrier of the product. 61. The product already has an item level product identifier. 62. The EU registry may keep a record of the state of each registered product. USER STORY 10: CHANGE DPPSP 63. The REO has a contract with a DPPSP, which is authorized to manage (several of) the DPP the REO is responsible for. 64. Both the former DPPSP and the new DPPSP make use of internationally recognized standards to make the transition technically feasible and economically viable. USER STORY 11: TRANSFER OF RESPONSIBLE ECONOMIC OPERATOR 65. Only one economic operator can be the REO under the ESPR for a product at a given time. 66. Updating or adding data to a DPP does not make it a ‘new DPP’ since it is expected that DPP data can be updated (e.g., state of health indicators) or that the DPP data will be enriched (e.g. inclusion of repair events.) 67. Transfer of ESPR-related legal responsibility does not necessarily lead to the creation of a ‘new DPP’ needing to be linked to the old DPP, as expressed in Article 10 d). 68. The transfer of ESPR-related legal responsibilities to a new REO can only occur if there exists a contractual agreement with the previous REO or if the previous REO has gone out of business. 94 69. Transfer of ESPR-related legal responsibility does not imply that the product should be registered a second time in the EU registry. 70. Transfer of ESPR-related legal responsibility between REO1 and REO2 can be such that REO1 retains no technical responsibility after having been released from its legal responsibility. 71. If the weblink (i.e., UID-enabled URI) is changed, the data carrier may also have to be changed to ensure continued access. 72. This user story is independent of the possibility that certain of REO1’s or REO2’s ESPR-related responsibilities might be delegated to DPPSP. 73. It is likely that creating ‘new DPPs’ for a product that already has one will add data quality risks and costs. Thus, the situations leading to the creation of a ‘new DPP’ linked to another DPP should be clearly identified (e.g., remanufacturing). 74. If a company or product line is taken over by a new company, transfer of ESPR-related legal responsibility may occur at any granularity level (model/batch/item), but for other scenarios, this transfer will likely only occur at item level. In particular, in the case of downstream circular economy operators wishing to voluntarily take over the ESPR-related legal responsibilities for a product, it is highly recommended that for product categories for which this user story may apply, there are requirements by delegated acts that all products are identified at item (instance) level when placed on the market, thus avoiding the addition of serialized identification by the downstream economic actor. 75. This user story does not address the situation where an economic operator may voluntarily take responsibility for a product in the absence of a contractual agreement with the REO, as the legal context surrounding this situation is not yet well understood. 76. If the operator identifier of the REO is stored in the EU registry, transfer of ESPR-related responsibility to another economic operator may require updating the EU registry. 77. With regards to the possibility of keeping track of the history of REOs, several technical solutions are possible to achieve this, we consider applying both: a. using the EU registry: REO1 modifies the existing EU registry entry to declare REO2 as the current and new REO for this product. b. by writing a change of responsibility event to the DPP to track the past and current REOs. This can maintain a chain of custody of the product. c. Similarly, since the responsibility for DPP data content is now with REO2, for traceability reasons, the change of responsibility event may need to be noted in the DPP. USER STORY 12: MAKE AVAILABLE A BACKED-UP DPP 78. An REO and DPPSP that provides back-ups have a business agreement 79. The DPPSP has some legal obligation to continue to provide the back-up service even after insolvency of the REO USER STORY 13: END OF DPP AVAILABILITY 80. An REO bears the cost of providing access to the DPP. The requirement from the ESPR (article 9.2i) states that the DPP must remain available for “…at least the expected lifetime of a specific product”. An REO may decide to stop providing access to the DPP for a product when the “expected lifetime” has expired. 95 81. The end of DPP availability means that the DPP can no longer be accessed, updated, or otherwise interacted with via the Web Portal, EU registry, and data carrier. The REO may still keep a copy of the DPP in their own systems for their needs. 82. An REO can mark/indicate their products’ DPP as unavailable in the EU registry after the DPP no longer have to be available. 96 APPENDIX F: CONSIDERATIONS FOR USER STORIES V4.0 Two open points have been raised during the writing of this document that could not be processed in the given timeframe. A future version of this document could take these into account and elaborate on them: • For completeness, consider a User Story in which an REO updates their own DPP. • Consider ‘non-happy flow’ user stories that deal with aspects such as, security, fraud, and data quality.
